_work.title: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: A cross-section of the foundational tarot decks of Italy and France—the hand-painted fifteenth-century Visconti and Estensi courts, the engraved Sola Busca and Mantegna series, and the woodcut Tarot de Marseille tradition (Dodal, Vieville, Madenié, Besançon)—completing the historic coverage of the deck-by-deck record.

entries.title: Queen of Swords — Visconti-Sforza Tarot
entries.creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
entries.summary:
The Queen of Swords (*regina di spade*) from the Visconti-Sforza tarocchi, a hand-painted Milanese deck attributed to Bonifacio Bembo, c. 1450–1480.

## Iconography
A seated or standing crowned queen holds an upright sword, set against the deck's characteristic gold-tooled ground and bearing the heraldry of the Visconti and Sforza houses. The court cards of this suit personify the rank of queen within the martial suit of swords.

## Significance
The Visconti-Sforza is the most complete and luxurious of the surviving fifteenth-century Italian *tarocchi*, made for the ruling family of Milan. Unlike later mass-produced woodcut decks, these were luxury miniatures. Court cards such as this queen show how the suit signs ([[motif:sword|swords]], cups, batons, coins) and the four court ranks predate the divinatory tarot and descend from earlier playing-card traditions.

## For artists & game designers
A model for a gold-ground court-card aesthetic—heraldry, permanence, and dynastic display rather than fortune-telling.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

entries.title: Jupiter — Tarot de Besançon (Trump V)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Besançon workshop)
entries.summary:
Jupiter, the trump that replaces the Pope (V) in the Tarot de Besançon, the Protestant-regional variant of the Tarot de Marseille.

## Iconography
The classical god Jupiter is enthroned with his eagle and thunderbolt, occupying the position the standard Marseille deck gives to *Le Pape* (the Hierophant). In the Besançon pattern the two ecclesiastical trumps—the Pope (V) and the Popess (II)—are swapped for the pagan deities Jupiter and Juno.

## Significance
The Besançon variant emerged in the largely Protestant and Imperial-leaning cardmaking centres of eastern France and the Rhineland, where the explicitly Catholic figures of Pope and Papess were unwelcome. Substituting [[motif:jupiter|Jupiter]] and Juno secularised the deck while keeping the trump count intact—a vivid case of confessional politics reshaping a divinatory iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A ready example of “reskinning” a card to dodge censorship while preserving game structure.

entries.title: Trump V (Jupiter / Hierophant) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
entries.creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
entries.summary:
Trump V of the Tarot de Besançon as printed by the Renault workshop, occupying the position the Marseille deck assigns to the Pope.

## Iconography
A woodcut, stencil-coloured trump in the Marseille manner, here from the Renault edition of the Besançon pattern. In this regional variant the fifth trump is Jupiter rather than *Le Pape*.

## Significance
The Renault firm of Besançon was among the long-lived French cardmakers who kept the Besançon pattern in production into the nineteenth century. The survival of complete Renault sheets lets scholars track how the [[motif:marseille pattern|Marseille template]] mutated regionally over two centuries.

## For artists & game designers
Note the flat stencil palette—red, blue, yellow, flesh—typical of cheap mass-produced French tarots.

entries.title: Trump XIII (Death) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
entries.creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
entries.summary:
The thirteenth trump, Death, from the Renault edition of the Tarot de Besançon.

## Iconography
A skeletal reaper swings a scythe through a field of severed heads, hands, and feet—the standard Marseille image of the “nameless arcanum.” In most Marseille decks the card carries no title.

## Significance
Death as a mowing skeleton fuses the medieval [[motif:memento mori|*memento mori*]] and the *danse macabre* with the tarot's allegorical sequence. Its placement at XIII, traditionally an unlucky number, and its frequent namelessness made it a focus of later esoteric speculation about transformation rather than literal death.

## For artists & game designers
The scattered crowned heads in the soil are the key detail—death levels king and commoner alike.

entries.title: Trump XVI (The Tower / La Maison-Dieu) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
entries.creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
entries.summary:
The sixteenth trump, the Tower (*La Maison-Dieu*), from the Renault Tarot de Besançon.

## Iconography
A crenellated tower is struck at the crown by a bolt or burst from the sun, its top blown off, with two figures tumbling head-first amid scattered coloured spheres.

## Significance
The French title *La Maison-Dieu* (“the House of God”) is debated—possibly a corruption, possibly a reference to a hospice or to the Tower of Babel. In the [[motif:marseille pattern|Marseille tradition]] it is the image of sudden catastrophe and divine overthrow; the occult revival later read it as the shattering of false structures.

## For artists & game designers
The falling bodies and exploding crown make this the most kinetic image in the trump sequence—pure disaster.

entries.title: Trump XXI (The World / Le Monde) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
entries.creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
entries.summary:
The twenty-first and highest trump, the World (*Le Monde*), from the Renault Tarot de Besançon.

## Iconography
A dancing nude or lightly draped figure stands within a laurel mandorla, framed in the four corners by the [[motif:tetramorph|tetramorph]]—the man/angel, eagle, lion, and ox of the four evangelists.

## Significance
The World closes the trump sequence as the image of completion and cosmic order. The surrounding tetramorph links the card to the visionary creatures of Ezekiel and Revelation and to the four elements; the central figure is variously read as the soul, the *anima mundi*, or Christ in glory.

## For artists & game designers
The mandorla-plus-four-corners layout is one of the oldest framing devices in Christian art—instantly legible as “totality.”

entries.title: The Chariot — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
entries.creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
entries.summary:
The Chariot from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) tarocchi, among the earliest surviving tarot cards, c. 1442–1447.

## Iconography
A crowned lady rides a triumphal car drawn by horses, on the deck's tooled gold ground. The triumph (*trionfo*) is the structural metaphor of the whole game—each trump a higher “triumph” in procession.

## Significance
The Cary-Yale deck, held at Yale's Beinecke Library, is unusual for its expanded structure—it includes the three theological virtues and female as well as male court ranks. As one of the three great Milanese hand-painted decks, it documents the tarot at its courtly origin, before standardisation. The [[motif:triumphal car|chariot]] derives directly from Petrarch's *Trionfi* and Renaissance triumphal pageantry.

## For artists & game designers
A primary source for the “triumph” as procession—the root metaphor behind the entire trump ladder.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

entries.title: Charity (Caritas) — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
entries.creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
entries.summary:
Charity (*Caritas*), one of the three theological-virtue trumps unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti tarocchi.

## Iconography
Charity is personified as a woman nursing or tending children, often with a flame or pelican, the standard attributes of caritas in Italian art.

## Significance
Standard tarot retains only the four cardinal virtues' subset (Justice, Strength/Fortitude, Temperance); the Cary-Yale deck additionally includes the three [[motif:theological virtue|theological virtues]] Faith, Hope, and Charity, evidence that early tarot structure was not yet fixed. Their presence suggests an originally moralising, didactic programme behind the trump sequence.

## For artists & game designers
Charity's nursing-mother type is the visual opposite of the deck's martial court cards—useful for an allegory of nurture.

entries.title: Faith (Fides) — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
entries.creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
entries.summary:
Faith (*Fides*), one of the three theological-virtue trumps unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti tarocchi.

## Iconography
Faith is personified as a woman holding a chalice and/or cross, the conventional attributes of *Fides* in Renaissance allegory, set on the gold ground.

## Significance
Faith, Hope, and Charity appear in the Cary-Yale deck but in no later standard tarot, making this deck a key witness to the fluid early state of the trump sequence. Their inclusion alongside the cardinal virtues points to a structured ladder of [[motif:theological virtue|virtues]] as part of the game's original moral architecture.

## For artists & game designers
Faith's chalice-and-cross attributes make her readable at a glance even without a caption.

entries.title: Hope (Spes) — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
entries.creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
entries.summary:
Hope (*Spes*), one of the three theological-virtue trumps unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti tarocchi.

## Iconography
Hope is shown as a woman, hands raised in prayer or reaching upward, sometimes with an anchor, looking toward heaven—the standard personification of *Spes*.

## Significance
Together with Faith and Charity, Hope rounds out the theological virtues that the Cary-Yale deck adds to the trump set. Their appearance only here—and never in the Tarot de Marseille line—demonstrates that the canonical 22-trump structure was a later consolidation of more varied early programmes. The [[motif:theological virtue|virtues]] anchor the game in late-medieval moral allegory.

## For artists & game designers
Hope's upward gaze and anchor are a compact emblem of aspiration—useful far beyond tarot.

entries.title: The Empress — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
entries.creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
entries.summary:
The Empress from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) tarocchi, c. 1442–1447.

## Iconography
A crowned and richly robed empress is seated in state, frequently holding an imperial shield bearing an eagle, on the deck's gold-tooled ground. She is the female sovereign of the trump procession, paired with the Emperor.

## Significance
In the Milanese decks the Empress and Emperor reflect the Visconti family's imperial pretensions; the heraldic eagle ties the card to dynastic propaganda as much as to allegory. As one of the earliest surviving Empress cards, it shows the [[motif:imperial|imperial]] iconography that would persist, simplified, all the way into the woodcut Marseille tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Note how heraldry doubles as characterisation—the shield tells you whose Empress this is.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

entries.title: The Emperor — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
entries.creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
entries.summary:
The Emperor from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) tarocchi, c. 1442–1447.

## Iconography
A bearded, crowned emperor sits enthroned holding an orb or sceptre, attended and set against gold, often with the imperial eagle. He embodies temporal sovereignty at the head of the secular trumps.

## Significance
The Emperor and Empress pair anchors the lower trumps in the order of worldly rule, above which rise the Pope and Popess (spiritual rule) and then the cosmic and eschatological cards. The Cary-Yale Emperor's [[motif:imperial|imperial regalia]] reflect the Visconti claim to dignities granted by the Holy Roman Emperor.

## For artists & game designers
The orb-and-throne formula is the universal shorthand for legitimate worldly power.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

entries.title: The Pope — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
entries.summary:
The Pope from the so-called “Charles VI” tarot, a late-fifteenth-century Ferrarese deck (Bibliothèque nationale de France).

## Iconography
The Pope sits enthroned in the papal tiara, raising a hand in blessing, attended by kneeling figures. He is the spiritual counterpart to the Emperor in the trump hierarchy.

## Significance
Long misattributed to a deck painted for King Charles VI of France in 1392, these seventeen surviving cards are now dated c. 1470–1480 and assigned to a [[motif:ferrara|Ferrarese]] workshop, possibly connected to the Este court. They form one of the most important non-Milanese witnesses to early Italian tarot iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A reminder that confident attributions can be wrong by a century—always check the redating.

entries.title: The Emperor — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
entries.summary:
The Emperor (*Empereur*) from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
A crowned emperor is enthroned holding a sceptre, the imperial eagle nearby, courtiers below. He heads the trumps of worldly authority.

## Significance
The seventeen “Charles VI” cards survive at the BnF and exemplify the high quality of [[motif:ferrara|Ferrarese]] card painting in the Este period. Comparison of this Emperor with the Milanese Visconti versions lets scholars map regional variation in the earliest trump iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A second-region take on the Emperor—useful for showing how the same allegory drifts between workshops.

entries.title: Death — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
entries.summary:
Death from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
Death appears as a skeleton—here often mounted or striding with a bow or scythe across fallen bodies—an early Italian version of the trump that later Marseille decks render as the nameless reaper.

## Significance
The Death trump links the tarot to the pan-European [[motif:memento mori|*memento mori*]] and *Triumph of Death* imagery popular in fifteenth-century Italy (compare the Palermo and Pisa frescoes). The Ferrarese rendering is among the earliest surviving tarot Deaths.

## For artists & game designers
Mounted, archer Death is a striking alternative to the standing scythe-bearer—more huntsman than mower.

entries.title: The Sun — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
entries.summary:
The Sun from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
A radiant sun-disc with a face shines above a figure below—in this early deck often a single woman holding a distaff, rather than the twin children of later Marseille versions.

## Significance
The celestial trumps (Star, Moon, Sun) form a luminous sequence near the top of the ladder. The Ferrarese Sun shows how variable these images were before standardisation: the [[motif:sol|solar disc]] is constant, but the figures beneath it differ markedly from deck to deck.

## For artists & game designers
The distaff-spinner under the sun is a quieter, more domestic image than the leaping children of the Marseille Sun.

entries.title: The Hermit (Time) — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
entries.summary:
The Hermit—here in its older guise as Time—from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
A bearded old man leans on crutches or a staff and holds an hourglass, the attribute of [[motif:father time|Time]] (*il Vecchio*, “the Old Man”). Only later did the lantern replace the hourglass and the figure become the lamp-bearing Hermit.

## Significance
This card preserves the trump's earliest meaning: not solitary wisdom but Time the devourer, one of Petrarch's *Trionfi*. The shift from hourglass to lantern over the following centuries is a textbook case of iconographic drift reshaping a card's meaning.

## For artists & game designers
Give your “hermit” an hourglass instead of a lamp and you instantly evoke the older, darker Time.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

entries.title: The World — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
entries.summary:
The World, the highest trump, from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
Two putti or figures hold up a great orb on which is painted a walled city in a landscape under heaven—the world entire, displayed as a globe. This differs sharply from the later mandorla-and-tetramorph Marseille World.

## Significance
The “Charles VI” World shows the medieval cosmographic image of the inhabited earth—a [[motif:globe|globe]] bearing a fortified city—as the culmination of the triumph sequence. Its survival is crucial evidence that the World card's iconography was once quite different across Italian regions.

## For artists & game designers
A city-on-a-globe held aloft by genii is a gorgeous, under-used emblem of “the whole world.”

entries.title: L'Empereur (The Emperor) — Jean Dodal Tarot de Marseille (IIII)
entries.creator: Jean Dodal
entries.summary:
L'Empereur (trump IIII) from the Jean Dodal Tarot de Marseille, printed in Lyon c. 1701–1715.

## Iconography
The Emperor sits in profile, legs crossed, holding a sceptre topped with an orb, an eagle-blazoned shield at his side—the canonical Marseille pose of worldly authority.

## Significance
The Dodal deck is one of the earliest and most influential exemplars of the “Tarot de Marseille type I” (TdM I) pattern. Its slightly archaic woodcut style and Italianate name-spellings make it a key witness to the standard French tarot before the Conver workshop fixed the “type II” standard. This entry extends the project's existing Dodal majors (Bateleur, Papesse, Chariot). The crossed-leg [[motif:marseille pattern|Emperor pose]] became iconic.

## For artists & game designers
The profile, crossed-legs Emperor is the signature Marseille posture—instantly “tarot.”

entries.title: Le Monde (The World) — Pierre Madenié Tarot de Marseille (XXI), 1709
entries.creator: Pierre Madenié
entries.summary:
Le Monde (trump XXI), the highest trump, from the dated 1709 Tarot de Marseille of Pierre Madenié of Dijon.

## Iconography
A dancing figure within a laurel mandorla is framed by the four creatures of the [[motif:tetramorph|tetramorph]] (angel, eagle, lion, ox), in the classic Marseille configuration.

## Significance
The Madenié deck of 1709 is one of the most important securely-dated early Marseille tarots and a benchmark for the “type II” (TdM II) pattern later codified by Nicolas Conver. Its precise date makes it an anchor point in the chronology of French tarot production. (This Commons image is a CC BY-SA restoration of the surviving deck.)

## For artists & game designers
A securely dated reference if you need a “1709 Marseille” look pinned to a real year.

entries.title: The Star — Jacques Vieville Tarot (XVII)
entries.creator: Jacques Vieville
entries.summary:
The Star (XVII) from the Jacques Vieville tarot, Paris, c. 1650.

## Iconography
The Vieville Star departs from the Marseille norm: instead of a kneeling woman pouring water under stars, it shows a standing figure (an astronomer or shepherd) gesturing toward a great star with a compass or staff.

## Significance
Vieville's deck, produced in Paris around the same time as the Noblet tarot, represents an alternative northern-French line that did not become the dominant Marseille standard. Its idiosyncratic trumps—like this [[motif:the star|Star]]—are invaluable for showing the diversity of seventeenth-century French tarot before standardisation around the Marseille pattern.

## For artists & game designers
An astronomer-with-compass Star is a fresh alternative to the ubiquitous water-pouring maiden.

entries.title: Imperator (The Emperor) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, National Gallery of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
*Imperator*, the Emperor, from the so-called Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series), engraved in Ferrara c. 1465.

## Iconography
A crowned emperor sits enthroned with orb and sceptre, attendants below, captioned IMPERATOR. He belongs to the deck's first decad, the “Conditions/Ranks of Man,” which ascends from Beggar to Pope to Emperor.

## Significance
Despite the name, the Mantegna Tarocchi is not a tarot and not by Mantegna: it is a fifty-card engraved encyclopedia of the cosmos in five decads—ranks of man, [[motif:muses|Apollo and the Muses]], the liberal arts, the cosmic principles/virtues, and the planets and spheres. It profoundly shaped Renaissance print culture and was copied across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
A model for an “encyclopedic” card set—every card a rung on a ladder from earth to the *primum mobile*.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters* (Mantegna Tarocchi).

entries.title: Misero (The Beggar) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Conditions of Man 1)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
*Misero*, the Beggar (no. 1, lowest of the Conditions of Man) in the Mantegna Tarocchi E-series.

## Iconography
A ragged beggar walks with a staff, beset by two dogs—the bottom rung of the social ladder, captioned MISERO. The decad rises from him through Servant, Artisan, Merchant, Gentleman, Knight, Doge, King, Emperor, to Pope.

## Significance
The Beggar opens the Mantegna sequence's [[motif:conditions of man|Conditions of Man]], a hierarchy of estates that mirrors the wider cosmic ladder of the deck. The same figure (as *Misero*) became the model for the Fool/Beggar in several later tarot traditions.

## For artists & game designers
The dogs nipping at the beggar are the detail that makes “lowest of the low” read instantly.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Re (The King) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Conditions of Man 8)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
*Re*, the King (no. 8 in the Conditions of Man), from the Mantegna Tarocchi E-series.

## Iconography
A crowned king is enthroned with sceptre and orb, captioned RE, just below the Emperor and Pope in the rank ladder.

## Significance
The King sits near the top of the [[motif:conditions of man|estates ladder]] that forms the first decad of the Mantegna series. The careful gradation of King, Emperor, and Pope reflects the late-medieval theory of the two swords—temporal and spiritual power—made visual.

## For artists & game designers
King-then-Emperor-then-Pope is a clean three-step escalation of authority for any ranked card set.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Temperantia (Temperance) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Cosmic Principles & Virtues 34)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
*Temperantia*, Temperance (no. 34), one of the virtues in the Mantegna Tarocchi's fourth decad of Cosmic Principles and Virtues.

## Iconography
A woman pours liquid between two vessels—the standard attribute of [[motif:temperance|Temperance]], the act of mixing and moderating—captioned TEMPERANTIA.

## Significance
The Mantegna series groups the cardinal and other virtues with cosmic principles such as Genius and the Muses, presenting morality as woven into the structure of the universe. This pouring-vessels image is the direct ancestor of the Temperance card in the tarot proper.

## For artists & game designers
Two vessels and a careful stream of water = “moderation” with no text required.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Genius of the World (Genius Mundi) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Cosmic Principles 33)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The Genius of the World (*Genius Mundi*, no. 33) from the Mantegna Tarocchi's decad of Cosmic Principles and Virtues.

## Iconography
A winged genius personifies the animating spirit of the cosmos, set among the abstract principles—Cosmico (cosmos), Tempo (time), and the virtues—that bridge the human ranks and the celestial spheres.

## Significance
The “Genius of the World” gives visual form to the Neoplatonic *anima mundi*, the world-soul that pervades and orders creation. Its inclusion shows how the Mantegna deck functions as a Renaissance cosmological diagram, ascending from [[motif:conditions of man|man]] through abstract principles to the planets and the *primum mobile*.

## For artists & game designers
A personified “world-spirit” is a perfect hub-figure for a cosmology-themed deck.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Sol (The Sun) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Firmaments of the Universe 44)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
*Sol*, the Sun (no. 44), from the Mantegna Tarocchi's fifth decad, the planets and firmaments of the universe.

## Iconography
The Sun is personified as the radiant solar deity—Apollo/Sol—driving a chariot across the heavens, captioned SOL. The decad runs through Moon, Mercury, Venus, Sol, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn to the Eighth Sphere, *Primum Mobile*, and *Prima Causa*.

## Significance
The planetary decad presents the [[motif:planets|seven planets]] in the Ptolemaic order as the upper rungs of the cosmic ladder. Personifying Sol as a charioteer follows classical and medieval planet-deity iconography that pervaded astrological art.

## For artists & game designers
The charioteer-Sun is the canonical way to draw the planet rather than the disc.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Rhetorica (Rhetoric) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Liberal Arts 23)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
*Rhetorica*, Rhetoric, one of the seven Liberal Arts in the third decad of the Mantegna Tarocchi.

## Iconography
Rhetoric is personified as a woman with attributes of eloquence—often a sword or scroll and a commanding gesture—captioned RHETORICA, among the arts of the trivium and quadrivium.

## Significance
The [[motif:liberal arts|Liberal Arts]] decad (Grammar, Logic, Rhetoric, Geometry, Arithmetic, Music, Poetry, Philosophy, Astrology, Theology) makes the medieval curriculum into a visual encyclopedia. The Mantegna images became standard models for personifying the arts in later Renaissance prints and decorative cycles.

## For artists & game designers
A full personification set of the liberal arts—ideal reference for an “academy” or scholarly faction.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Geometria (Geometry) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Liberal Arts 24)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Metropolitan Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
*Geometria* (XXIIII), Geometry, from the Liberal Arts decad of the Mantegna Tarocchi.

## Iconography
Geometry is personified as a woman holding a compass and measuring instruments, often bending to measure the ground—the attribute of the geometer—captioned GEOMETRIA.

## Significance
Geometry belongs to the quadrivium, the mathematical half of the [[motif:liberal arts|seven liberal arts]]. The compass-wielding figure ties the card to the Renaissance ideal of the universe as measurable and the artist-engineer as measurer—an image that resonates from Mantegna through to later Masonic and esoteric uses of the compass.

## For artists & game designers
The compass-and-ground-measuring pose is the visual root of countless later “architect/geometer” emblems.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Charita (Charity) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Genii & Virtues 38)
entries.creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
entries.summary:
*Charita*, Charity (plate 38), among the Genii and Virtues of the Mantegna Tarocchi.

## Iconography
Charity is personified holding a flame or tending children, the standard attributes of *caritas*, the highest of the theological virtues.

## Significance
Charity appears in the same decad as the cardinal virtues and the cosmic genii, integrating the [[motif:theological virtue|theological virtues]] into the deck's cosmological scheme. The Mantegna virtue figures were widely copied and helped standardise virtue iconography across Renaissance Europe.

## For artists & game designers
Flame-bearing Charity makes a warm counterweight to the colder cardinal virtues in a virtue set.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

entries.title: Panfilio — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump I)
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
entries.summary:
*Panfilio*, trump I of the Sola Busca Tarot, the earliest surviving complete 78-card deck, engraved in northern Italy c. 1491.

## Iconography
A named figure (Panfilio) stands in the deck's fine line-engraved, hand-coloured style. Unlike the Marseille trumps, the Sola Busca trumps depict named classical, Roman, and biblical heroes rather than the standard allegories.

## Significance
The Sola Busca is the only fifteenth-century deck to survive complete, and the only one whose pip cards bear full scenic illustrations. Its [[motif:alchemy|alchemical]] and antiquarian programme sets it utterly apart; A. E. Waite and Pamela Colman Smith studied British Museum photographs of it when designing the scenic minor arcana of the 1909 Rider-Waite-Smith deck.

## For artists & game designers
Named-hero trumps turn an abstract allegory deck into a roster of characters—great for a story-driven set.

citations.text: Sofia Di Vincenzo, *Sola-Busca Tarot* (1998); British Museum impressions.

entries.title: Postumio — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump II)
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
entries.summary:
*Postumio*, trump II of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), one of the named-hero trumps that replace the standard Popess.

## Iconography
A classical/Roman figure named Postumio stands in martial dress in the deck's engraved style. The Sola Busca trumps form a gallery of antique worthies rather than the Marseille allegories.

## Significance
The substitution of Roman and biblical heroes (Postumio, Catone, Nerone, Nabuchodenasor, and others) for the conventional trumps marks the Sola Busca as a humanist and antiquarian project, plausibly carrying an [[motif:alchemy|alchemical]] subtext, made for a learned patron rather than for ordinary play.

## For artists & game designers
Replacing each trump with a historical figure is a clean way to theme a deck around a single culture.

entries.title: Sesto — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump VI)
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
entries.summary:
*Sesto*, trump VI of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), part of its gallery of named antique heroes.

## Iconography
A classical warrior-figure named Sesto stands in the deck's distinctive engraved manner, occupying the position the standard deck assigns to the Lovers.

## Significance
The Sola Busca's reassignment of subjects—here a Roman *Sesto* in place of the Lovers—demonstrates how freely the trump programme could be reimagined in fifteenth-century Italy. The cycle's coherence and its dense [[motif:alchemy|symbolism]] have led some scholars to read it as an alchemical-initiatic sequence.

## For artists & game designers
The engraved hatching and antique armour give a cool, antiquarian alternative to the bright woodcut tarots.

entries.title: Bocho — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump XIV)
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
entries.summary:
*Bocho*, trump XIV of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), one of its named-hero trumps.

## Iconography
A named figure, *Bocho* (often identified with the Numidian king Bocchus), stands in the engraved Sola Busca style where the standard deck shows Temperance.

## Significance
The Sola Busca trumps draw on Roman history and the Jugurthine War among other sources, turning the trump ladder into a parade of antiquity. The depth of its learned programme and possible [[motif:alchemy|alchemical]] coding make it the single most studied early deck after the Visconti-Sforza.

## For artists & game designers
Obscure historical names invite worldbuilding—each card is a hook for a backstory.

entries.title: Ipeo — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump XVII)
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
entries.summary:
Trump XVII (named *Ipeo*) of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), occupying the position the standard deck gives to the Star.

## Iconography
A named classical figure stands or rides in the Sola Busca's fine engraved manner, again replacing the conventional celestial allegory with an antique personage.

## Significance
Because the Sola Busca substitutes named heroes for the upper trumps as well as the lower, its sequence severs the usual link between trump number and standard image—a vivid demonstration that the “canonical” 22 trumps were not yet fixed. Its [[motif:alchemy|engraved symbolism]] later fascinated the founders of the Golden Dawn.

## For artists & game designers
Decoupling card position from expected image keeps players guessing—useful for a deck with hidden lore.

entries.title: Knight of Swords — Sola Busca Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
entries.summary:
The Knight of Swords from the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), British Museum impression.

## Iconography
A mounted knight brandishes a sword, rendered in fine line-engraving. The Sola Busca court cards, like its trumps, are individualised antique-style figures.

## Significance
The Sola Busca is the earliest deck whose [[motif:suit of swords|suit and court cards]] are fully and individually illustrated, not merely arranged repetitions of the suit sign. This scenic treatment of the minor arcana was unique until the British Museum's photographs inspired Pamela Colman Smith's fully illustrated pips four centuries later.

## For artists & game designers
The Sola Busca proves that even “number” and court cards can each carry a unique scene.

citations.text: Sofia Di Vincenzo, *Sola-Busca Tarot* (1998); British Museum.

entries.title: Seven of Discs (Coins) — Sola Busca Tarot
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photograph of PD engraving). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The Seven of Discs (Coins) from the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), a fully illustrated pip card.

## Iconography
Rather than seven coins arranged decoratively, the card stages a small scene incorporating the seven discs—the hallmark of the Sola Busca's [[motif:scenic pip|scenic pips]].

## Significance
The Sola Busca is the only fifteenth-century deck with scenic illustrations on every numbered card. This Seven of Discs is among the pips whose composition has been compared with the Rider-Waite-Smith minor arcana, the deck that made scenic pips standard in modern tarot. (The Commons image is a CC BY-SA photograph of the public-domain engraving.)

## For artists & game designers
A direct fifteenth-century precedent for telling a tiny story on a “number” card.
